---
title: "Pop Culture and Political Polarization in America: Billboard Hot 100 Trends Over Time"
author: "Matthew Reiling, Suler Lu, Bella Vicencio"
format:
  dashboard:
    logo: "https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/a/a5/Billboard_Hot_100_logo_%282025_March_design%29.png"
    theme: lux
    embed-resources: true
    expandable: false
    scrolling: true
---

# Topic Introduction

::::: columns
::: card
## Topical Overview

Music and politics are both powerful parts of culture, and they often reflect what is happening in society at a given time. In this project, we explore how trends in popular music might relate to political changes in the United States. Using historical Billboard chart data and political polarization data, we examine how the popularity of different music genres changes over time and whether those changes align with shifts in political polarization. Specifically, our analysis focuses on three main research questions:

1.  **How has the number of #1 Rock songs on the Billboard Hot 100 changed over time?**
2.  **How do trends in popular music genres relate to changes in political polarization in the United States?**
3.  **Which music genres (Rock, Pop, Funk, Hip Hop, and Electronic) show the strongest correlation with political polarization trends?**

Understanding the connection between music and politics is important because music has often been used to express social movements and public opinion. Genres like rock and hip hop have historically been associated with protest, identity, and cultural change. For example, rock music played a major role in youth culture and political expression during the 1960s and 1970s. Music has long served as a reflection of social tensions and political debate in the United States. More information about the cultural influence of rock music can be found through the Rock & Roll Hall of Fame: <https://rockhall.com/inductees/classes/>. By examining how music popularity changes during different political climates, we can better understand how culture and politics influence each other.

To answer our research questions, we combine two data sets. The first data set contains information about songs that reached number one on the Billboard Hot 100 chart, which measures the most popular songs in the United States based on streaming, radio airplay, and sales. The official Billboard chart archives can be accessed here:<https://www.billboard.com/charts/hot-100/>. For our analysis, we filtered the data set to focus on specific genres such as Rock, Pop, Funk, Hip Hop, and Electronic. The second data set measures political polarization in the United States and comes from the Varieties of Democracy (V-Dem) project, which is available through Our World in Data: <https://ourworldindata.org/grapher/political-polarization-score>. This data set provides yearly estimates of political polarization, allowing us to compare music trends and political trends across the same time period.

This analysis is interesting because it looks at culture and politics together rather than studying them separately. Music is often used as a way for people to express their views, emotions, and reactions to social issues. If certain genres become more popular during periods of higher political polarization, it could suggest that music reflects broader societal tensions or cultural shifts. Even if there is not a strong relationship, exploring these patterns can help us understand how entertainment trends and political climates evolve over time.

There are also some limitations and ethical considerations when working with these data sets. The Billboard data set only includes songs that reached number one on the charts, which means it does not represent all music or all artists. This may overlook smaller artists or genres that were culturally important but did not reach the top of the charts. Additionally, the political polarization data measures national trends and does not capture regional or demographic differences within the United States. Because of these limitations, our results should be interpreted carefully. The goal of this project is not to claim that politics directly causes changes in music, but rather to explore possible patterns and relationships between cultural and political trends.
:::

::: card
## Data Context

![](images/clipboard-1814849386.webp)

**Billboard Hot 100 #1 Songs**

Dataset Source & Link: <https://www.billboard.com/charts/hot-100/>

Creator: Chris Dalla Riva, compiled in the book Uncharted Territory (2025)

This dataset includes all songs that reached number one on the Billboard Hot 100 chart in the U.S. For our project, we focused on Rock songs at first, but we later explored other genres like Pop, Funk, Hip Hop, and Electronic to see how trends might differ across styles. Each row shows one song that hit number one, along with the artist, title, date it charted, and genre. The data covers the years 1958 through early 2025, giving us a long view of how music popularity changed over time.

Billboard determines the number one songs based on a mix of streaming, radio play, and sales. Each row represents a song that reached number one in a given week. We counted how many songs from each genre reached the top spot each year, which lets us compare yearly popularity trends.

After cleaning, the Rock dataset contains 463 rows. The dataset is U.S.-specific, so it reflects American listening habits rather than global trends. The main advantage of this sample is that it shows the biggest hits consistently over decades, but it leaves out songs that didn’t reach number one, which could overlook niche or emerging trends.

**Political Polarization Score (V-Dem / Our World in Data)**

Dataset Source & Link: <https://archive.ourworldindata.org/20260204-165456/grapher/political-polarization-score.html>

Creator: V-Dem Institute, co-published by Our World in Data; data produced by Bastian Herre, Lucas Rodés-Guirao, and Esteban Ortiz-Ospina (2013)

This dataset measures political polarization in the U.S. over time, showing how political differences affect social interactions and civic life. Each row represents a single year with a score for polarization. We’re using it to explore whether changes in music trends, like the popularity of different genres, align with shifts in political polarization. The dataset spans 125 years, giving a big-picture view of long-term trends.

Polarization is measured using expert assessments of U.S. political institutions and social divisions. These assessments are combined into a yearly score, giving a consistent measure to compare across time.

The dataset has 125 rows, one for each year measured. It only provides a national-level view, so it doesn’t capture regional or local differences. Still, it’s useful for seeing broad patterns over decades, which fits well with our yearly music data.
:::
:::::

# Building Data {scrolling="true"}

## tabs {.tabset}

### Loading Data

::: {.rows fill="false"}
```{r}
#| title: Loading Data and Libraries
#| echo: true
#| expandable: false

library(tidyverse)
library(patchwork)
library(plotly)
library(DT)

billboard_hot_100_number_ones_df <-read.csv("~/Documents/INFO 201/Final Project/Billboard Hot 100 Number Ones Database - Data.csv")
polarization_score_df <- read.csv("~/Documents/INFO 201/Final Project/political-polarization-score.csv")
```

Here we load our necessary libraries and the data sets that we will be working with.
:::

### Data Joining

::: {.rows fill="false"}
We plan to join this data set with a political polarization data set using the variable Year.

In the original Billboard data set, songs are recorded with a specific chart date. To prepare for the join, we extracted the year from the full date variable. This allows us to group songs by year and count how many rock songs reached number one in each year. To ensure the join is successful, we needed to:

1.  Convert the date variable into a proper date format
2.  Extract the year consistently
3.  Make sure the year variable is stored in the same format in both data sets, such as numeric

Since both data sets use calendar years, the join is straightforward once the formatting is consistent.

Some more things to consider when joining were which years should or should not be included in our joined data set? Specifically, should we include years when there are zero #1 hit rock songs and what about years included in the political polarization data set not included in the Billboard data set? Well to answer our first question, yes we do want to include years with zero #1 hit rock songs because this although there are zero songs, this is still data that tells us valuable information. On the second question, no, we do not want to include years that both data sets do not have data on because then we would have no music information to compare our political polarization score to, making these data useless. Finally, after all of this we have a complete joined data set.

```{r}
#| echo: true
#| expandable: false

# First we will set up the dataframes needed for the Rock Genre. 
# Clean the data to only save what is relevant

billboard_hot_100_number_ones_df <- billboard_hot_100_number_ones_df[, c("Artist", "Song", "Date", "Discogs.Genre")]

# Only pull out songs with Rock as apart of the genre

rock_genre_songs_df <- billboard_hot_100_number_ones_df |>
  filter(str_detect(Discogs.Genre, "Rock"))

# Adding a year column to rock_genre_songs_df to be able to join it with our other dataset

rock_genre_songs_df <- rock_genre_songs_df |> mutate(Year = year(parse_date_time2(Date, "dmy", cutoff_2000 = 26)))

# Grouping the number of rock songs that charted #1 by each individual year

rock_songs_by_year <- rock_genre_songs_df |> group_by(Year) |> summarize(count = n())

# Clean our new data set by using filter to only extract the polarization score from the USA.

polarization_score_df <- polarization_score_df |> filter(code == "USA")
polarization_score_df <- polarization_score_df[, c("year", "v2cacamps__estimate_best")]

# Join our two data sets together by year

joined_df <- right_join(rock_genre_songs_df, polarization_score_df, join_by(Year == year))
joined_df <- joined_df |> filter(Year >= 1958)
glimpse(joined_df)

glimpse(billboard_hot_100_number_ones_df)

colnames(joined_df)

#The joined dataset contains the following columns: Artist, Song, Date, CDR. Genre, Year, v2cacamps\_\_estimate_best

nrow(joined_df)

#Below is the complete joined dataset for the Rock Music Genre

joined_df |> datatable(joined_df)
```
:::

### Data Observations

::: {.rows fill="false"}
Before cleaning and filtering for rock songs, the data set included over 1,000 rows. After cleaning, there were much fewer rows as we now only had rock songs. Similarly, there were over 100 columns before cleaning, but we reduced it to just a few key ones.

Important columns include:

-   song or title as character
-   artist as character
-   date as date
-   genre as character

If we described it similarly to using glimpse in R, it would look like this:

-   Rows: 1,175
-   Columns: 100 plus
-   Example structure:
    -   song <chr>
    -   artist <chr>
    -   date <date>
    -   genre <chr>

Each row represents a song that reached number one on the chart at a specific time. After grouping by year, the structure changes to something like:

-   Year <int>
-   rock_hits <int>

Each row then represents one year and the number of rock songs that reached number one in that year.
:::

### Data Issues

::: {.rows fill="false"}
There are a few minor data quality issues. Some genre labels may not be perfectly consistent. For example, some songs could be labeled as “Rock,” “Alternative Rock,” or another variation. This means we had to carefully filter the genre column to make sure we were capturing the correct songs.

One problem that we were experiencing when joining was the date formatting for our Billboard data set. This data set only provided a date (day-month-year) column and the political polarization data set had just the year. Furthermore, the year in the provided date column was a 2 digit number, leaving out the century. This is not a problem when read by humans, but for a computer, trying to decide which century a year should belong to is a little more difficult. While trying to extract the year out of the date, we ran into an issue where years before 69 were being labeled in the 2000s instead of the 1900s like we would expect. A bit of manipulation and manual intervention eventually got us the result we were looking for however.

Overall, the key variables such as date and genre are mostly complete and usable. Since the data comes from Billboard’s official chart records, the ranking and date information is considered highly reliable. The small formatting issues do not reduce the credibility of the data set.
:::

### Biases and Considerations

There are several potential biases in this combined data set due to what information is missing. The Billboard data set only includes songs that reached number one, which leaves out many artists and genres that may better reflect diverse cultural and social experiences. This can limit fairness and non-discrimination because certain groups and perspectives are underrepresented. The political polarization data is measured at the national level and does not account for differences across regions or communities, which makes it harder to fully explain or interpret trends. The data set also lacks demographic and socioeconomic details, such as race, income, or access to media, which reduces transparency and explainability. While privacy and safety are not major concerns since the data is aggregated and public, accountability and professional responsibility still matter because conclusions could be oversimplified. Overall, the data set only partially supports the promotion of human values and requires careful interpretation and human judgment.

### Combined Data Wrangling

::: {.rows fill="false"}
Now, we will perform at least three of the following cleaning or exploration methods on our combined data set.

```{r}
#| echo: true
#| expandable: false
#| height: content

#Create one new numerical column to count the number of rock songs per year in the Joined Data Frame.

rock_song_count_df <- joined_df |> 
  group_by(Year) |>
  summarise(rock_song_count = sum(grepl("Rock", Discogs.Genre), na.rm = TRUE))
```

```{r}
#| echo: true
#| expandable: false

#Join "polarization_score_df" with "rock_song_count_df" to add the new column for billboard top 100 Rock Genre song count per year appeared.

rock_genre_joined_df <- inner_join(rock_song_count_df, polarization_score_df, join_by(Year == year))
glimpse(rock_genre_joined_df)
```

1.  The first data wrangling step created was to make a new numerical column to count the number of rock songs per year in the Joined Data Frame. This step is useful because it helps to investigate what patterns or changes over time occurred between popular Rock genre songs and political history.
2.  The second step was to create a clean Joined Data Frame by cleaning up rows of data to remove what is irrelevant for our project question. For this step we only selected what would help inform graphs to be created.
3.  The third task was to manipulate the "joined_df" to add the new column for billboard top 100 Rock Genre song count per year. Without this new column, any data visualizations produced could be missing the analysis of relevant patterns.
4.  The next action taken was to clean the new revised data frame to only display one row per year and to delete repetitive count rows. This step was necessary because in order to make a concise and clear graph, there could not be repetition, since displaying a data point more than once which cause inaccuracies.
:::

# Interpreting Data {scrolling="true"}

## Question tabset {.tabset}

### Research Question 1

:::::: rows
#### Rock graphs tabset {.tabset}

##### Graph 1

::: columns
```{r}
#| echo: true
rock_bar_chart <- ggplot(rock_genre_joined_df,
               aes(x = Year,
                   y = rock_song_count)) +
  geom_col(fill = "darkblue") +
  labs(title = "Number of Rock Songs Per Year",
       y = "Number of Rock Songs") +
  theme_minimal()
```

```{r}
#| expandable: true
ggplotly(rock_bar_chart)
```
:::

##### Graphs 2 and 3

::: columns
```{r}
#| echo: true
# Scatter plot of points with x/y axis that plot Year on the
# X axis and the count of rock songs on the Y axis. The
# patchwork library tool is used to display the graphs next
# to each other. Finally we will make a graph based on our
# rock genre joined data

rock_scatterplot_1 <- ggplot(rock_genre_joined_df,
               aes(x = Year,
                   y = rock_song_count)) +
  geom_line(color = "darkblue") +
  geom_point(color = "darkblue") +
  labs(title = "Rock Song Count",
       y = "Number of Rock Songs")

rock_scatterplot_1 <- ggplotly(rock_scatterplot_1)

rock_scatterplot_2 <- ggplot(rock_genre_joined_df,
               aes(x = Year,
                   y = v2cacamps__estimate_best)) +
  geom_line(color = "lightblue") +
  geom_point(color = "lightblue") +
  labs(title = "V2 Estimate",
       y = "Political Polarization Score")

rock_scatterplot_2 <- ggplotly(rock_scatterplot_2)
```

```{r}
#| expandable: true

# stacked vertically
rock_scatterplot_1
rock_scatterplot_2
```
:::

##### Graph 4

::: columns
```{r}
#| echo: true
r1G4 <- ggplot(rock_genre_joined_df,
               aes(x = v2cacamps__estimate_best,
                   y = rock_song_count)) +
  geom_point(color = "darkblue") +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm") +
  labs(title = "Billboard Top 100 Number One Rock Songs vs Political Polarization",
       x = "Political Polarization Score",
       y = "Number of Rock Songs")
```

```{r}
#| expandable: true
ggplotly(r1G4)
```
:::

#### Description

Two different graphs were created based on our rock genre joined data frame. Two graphs were created because we are still exploring what the best option for data visualization is. So, for variety we kept a scatter plot and bar chart from the graphs we tried to make. Using geom_smooth, we were able to draw a line of best fit to demonstrate the relationship visualized on the scatter plot and better show the trend.
::::::

### Research Question 2

:::::: {.rows fill="false"}
```{r}
#| echo: true
#| height: content
# Apply the same steps from the Rock genre data cleaning and data joining to the Pop genre. This is to help compare the count of Pop
# genre songs and political polarization

pop_genre_songs_df <- billboard_hot_100_number_ones_df |> filter(str_detect(Discogs.Genre, "Pop"))
pop_genre_songs_df <- pop_genre_songs_df |> mutate(Year = year(parse_date_time2(Date,
                                                                                "dmy",
                                                                                cutoff_2000 = 26)))
pop_polarization_df <- right_join(pop_genre_songs_df, polarization_score_df, join_by(Year == year))
pop_polarization_df <- pop_polarization_df |> filter(Year >= 1958)

# join count of pop songs per year and political polarization
pop_songs_by_year <- pop_genre_songs_df |>
  group_by(Year) |> summarize(count = n())

pop_count_polarization_df <- right_join(pop_songs_by_year, polarization_score_df, join_by(Year == year)) |>
  filter(Year >= 1958)
pop_count_polarization_df <- pop_count_polarization_df |> replace_na(list(count = 0))
```

#### Pop graphs tabset {.tabset}

##### Graph 1

::: columns
```{r}
#| echo: true
pop_bar_chart <- ggplot(pop_count_polarization_df,
               aes(x = Year,
                   y = count)) +
  geom_col(fill = "hotpink2") +
  labs(title = "Number of Pop Songs Per Year",
       y = "Number of Pop Songs") +
  theme_minimal()
```

```{r}
#| expandable: true

ggplotly(pop_bar_chart)
```
:::

##### Graphs 2 and 3

::: columns
```{r}
#| echo: true
pop_scatterplot_1 <- ggplot(pop_count_polarization_df,
               aes(x = Year,
                   y = count)) +
  geom_line(color = "hotpink2") +
  geom_point(color = "hotpink2") +
  labs(title = "Pop Song Count",
       y = "Number of Pop Songs")

pop_scatterplot_1 <- ggplotly(pop_scatterplot_1)

pop_scatterplot_2 <- ggplot(pop_count_polarization_df,
               aes(x = Year,
                   y = v2cacamps__estimate_best)) +
  geom_line(color = "lightpink") +
  geom_point(color = "lightpink") +
  labs(title = "V2 Estimate",
       y = "Political Polarization Score")

pop_scatterplot_2 <- ggplotly(pop_scatterplot_2)
```

```{r}
#| expandable: true
pop_scatterplot_1
pop_scatterplot_2   # stacked vertically
```
:::

##### Graph 4

::: columns
```{r}
#| echo: true
r2G4 <- ggplot(pop_count_polarization_df,
               aes(x = v2cacamps__estimate_best,
                   y = count)) +
  geom_point(color = "hotpink2") +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", color = "lightpink") +
  labs(title = "Billboard Top 100 Number One Pop Songs vs Political Polarization",
       x = "Political Polarization Score",
       y = "Number of Pop Songs")
```

```{r}
#| expandable: true
ggplotly(r2G4)
```
:::
::::::

### Research Question 3

[Which music genres (Rock, Pop, Funk, Hip Hop, and Electronic) show the strongest correlation with political polarization trends in the United States?]{style="font-size: 32px; font-weight: bold;"}

#### Genre tabset {.tabset}

##### Funk

:::: {.rows fill="false"}
```{r}
#| echo: true
# Find the distribution of song count and correlation with political polarization for the other top three genres: Funk, Hip Hop, and Electronic

funk_genre_songs_df <- billboard_hot_100_number_ones_df |>
  filter(str_detect(Discogs.Genre, "Funk"))
funk_genre_songs_df <- funk_genre_songs_df |> mutate(Year = year(parse_date_time2(Date, "dmy", cutoff_2000 = 26)))
funk_polarization_df <- right_join(funk_genre_songs_df, polarization_score_df, join_by(Year == year))
funk_polarization_df <- funk_polarization_df |> filter(Year >= 1958)
funk_songs_by_year <- funk_genre_songs_df |> group_by(Year) |> summarize(count = n())

funk_count_polarization_df <- right_join(funk_songs_by_year, polarization_score_df, join_by(Year == year)) |> filter(Year >= 1958)
funk_count_polarization_df <- funk_count_polarization_df |> replace_na(list(count = 0))
```

::: columns
```{r}
#| echo: true
#| expandable: true
funk1<- ggplot(funk_count_polarization_df, aes(x = Year, y = count)) +
  geom_col(fill = "green4") +
  labs(title = "Number of Funk Songs Per Year",
       y = "Number of Funk Songs") +
  theme_minimal()

ggplotly(funk1)
```

```{r}
#| echo: true
#| expandable: true
funk2<- ggplot(funk_count_polarization_df, aes(x = v2cacamps__estimate_best, y = count)) +
  geom_point(color = "green4") +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", color = "green2") +
  labs(title = "Billboard Top 100 Number One Funk Songs vs Political Polarization",
       x = "Political Polarization Score",
       y = "Number of Funk Songs")

ggplotly(funk2)
```
:::
::::

##### Electronic

:::: {.rows fill="false"}
```{r}
#| echo: true
electronic_genre_songs_df <- billboard_hot_100_number_ones_df |>
  filter(str_detect(Discogs.Genre, "Electronic"))
electronic_genre_songs_df <- electronic_genre_songs_df |> mutate(Year = year(parse_date_time2(Date, "dmy", cutoff_2000 = 26)))
electronic_polarization_df <- right_join(electronic_genre_songs_df, polarization_score_df, join_by(Year == year))
electronic_polarization_df <- electronic_polarization_df |> filter(Year >= 1958)
electronic_songs_by_year <- electronic_genre_songs_df |> group_by(Year) |> summarize(count = n())

electronic_count_polarization_df <- right_join(electronic_songs_by_year, polarization_score_df, join_by(Year == year)) |> filter(Year >= 1958)
electronic_count_polarization_df <- electronic_count_polarization_df |> replace_na(list(count = 0))
```

::: columns
```{r}
#| echo: true
#| expandable: true
electronic1<- ggplot(electronic_count_polarization_df, aes(x = Year, y = count)) +
  geom_col(fill = "mediumpurple2") +
  labs(title = "Number of Electronic Songs Per Year",
       y = "Number of Electronic Songs") +
  theme_minimal()

ggplotly(electronic1)
```

```{r}
#| echo: true
#| expandable: true
electronic2<- ggplot(electronic_count_polarization_df, aes(x = v2cacamps__estimate_best, y = count)) +
  geom_point(color = "mediumpurple2") +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", color = "purple4") +
  labs(title = "Billboard Top 100 Number One Electronic Songs vs Political Polarization",
       x = "Political Polarization Score",
       y = "Number of Electronic Songs")

ggplotly(electronic2)
```
:::
::::

##### Hip Hop

:::: {.rows fill="false"}
```{r}
#| echo: true
hiphop_genre_songs_df <- billboard_hot_100_number_ones_df |>
  filter(str_detect(Discogs.Genre, "Hip Hop"))
hiphop_genre_songs_df <- hiphop_genre_songs_df |> mutate(Year = year(parse_date_time2(Date, "dmy", cutoff_2000 = 26)))
hiphop_polarization_df <- right_join(hiphop_genre_songs_df, polarization_score_df, join_by(Year == year))
hiphop_polarization_df <- hiphop_polarization_df |> filter(Year >= 1958)
hiphop_songs_by_year <- hiphop_genre_songs_df |> group_by(Year) |> summarize(count = n())

hiphop_count_polarization_df <- right_join(hiphop_songs_by_year, polarization_score_df, join_by(Year == year)) |> filter(Year >= 1958)
hiphop_count_polarization_df <- hiphop_count_polarization_df |> replace_na(list(count = 0))
```

::: columns
```{r}
#| echo: true
#| expandable: true
hiphop1<- ggplot(hiphop_count_polarization_df, aes(x = Year, y = count)) +
  geom_col(fill = "salmon") +
  labs(title = "Number of Hip Hop Songs Per Year",
       y = "Number of Hip Hop Songs") +
  theme_minimal()

ggplotly(hiphop1)
```

```{r}
#| echo: true
#| expandable: true
hiphop2<- ggplot(hiphop_count_polarization_df, aes(x = v2cacamps__estimate_best, y = count)) +
  geom_point(color = "salmon") +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", color = "red3") +
  labs(title = "Billboard Top 100 Number One Hip Hop Songs vs Political Polarization",
       x = "Political Polarization Score",
       y = "Number of Hip Hop Songs")

ggplotly(hiphop2)
```
:::
::::

#### Combining Genre Data

:::: {.rows fill="false"}
[Combining Genre Data]{style="font-size: 24px; font-weight: bold;"}

```{r}
#| echo: true
# Create a new dataframe that combines song counts from different genres and the political polarization score by year

genre_counts_df <- rock_genre_joined_df |>
  
  # Select the columns we need from the rock dataset
  select(Year, rock_song_count, v2cacamps__estimate_best) |>
  
  # Rename columns to make them easier to read
  rename(
    Rock = rock_song_count,
    Polarization = v2cacamps__estimate_best
  ) |>
  
  # Join the Pop genre counts by matching the Year column
  left_join(
    pop_count_polarization_df |>
      select(Year, count) |>
      rename(Pop = count),
    by = "Year"
  ) |>
  
  # Join the Funk genre counts
  left_join(
    funk_count_polarization_df |>
      select(Year, count) |>
      rename(Funk = count),
    by = "Year"
  ) |>
  
  # Join the Electronic genre counts
  left_join(
    electronic_count_polarization_df |>
      select(Year, count) |>
      rename(Electronic = count),
    by = "Year"
  ) |>
  
  # Join the Hip Hop genre counts
  left_join(
    hiphop_count_polarization_df |>
      select(Year, count) |>
      rename(HipHop = count),
    by = "Year"
  )

# Replace any missing values (NA) with 0
# This ensures that years without #1 songs in a genre are counted as zero instead of missing
genre_counts_df <- genre_counts_df |>
  replace_na(list(
    Rock = 0,
    Pop = 0,
    Funk = 0,
    Electronic = 0,
    HipHop = 0
  ))

# Display the structure of the dataframe to check that the variables and data types look correct
glimpse(genre_counts_df)

```

::: columns
```{r}
#| echo: true
# Create a correlation matrix for the genre counts and
# polarization score
cor_matrix <- genre_counts_df |>
  
# Remove the Year column because it is not a variable we
# want to calculate correlations for
select(-Year) |>
  
# Calculate the correlation between all remaining numeric
# variables "complete.obs" means the function will only use
# rows where all values are present
  cor(use = "complete.obs")
```

```{r}
# Display the correlation matrix
cor_matrix
```
:::

```{r}
#| echo: true
#| height: content

# Convert the correlation matrix into a dataframe so it can be manipulated with tidyverse tools
cor_long <- as.data.frame(cor_matrix) |>
  
# Create a new column called Genre1 that stores the row names from the correlation matrix
# This represents the first variable being compared in the correlation
  mutate(Genre1 = rownames(cor_matrix)) |>
  
# Reshape the data from wide format to long format
# Each row will now represent one pair of variables and their correlation value
  pivot_longer(
    -Genre1,                 # Keep Genre1 as a fixed column
    names_to = "Genre2",     # Create a new column with the second variable name
    values_to = "Correlation" # Store the correlation value for the pair
  )
```

```{r}
#| echo: true
#| height: content

# Square the correlation value to get an accurate R-squared value to better represent our correlation
cor_long <- cor_long |> mutate(Correlation = Correlation^2)
```

```{r}
#| echo: true
#| height: content
#| expandable: true

# Create a heatmap showing the correlation between music genres and political polarization
heatmap <- ggplot(cor_long, aes(x = Genre1, y = Genre2, fill = Correlation)) +
  
# Draw square tiles for each pair of variables in the correlation matrix
geom_tile(color = "white") +
  
# Set the color scale for the heatmap
# Blue represents negative correlation, red represents positive correlation,
# and purple in the middle represents values close to zero
scale_fill_gradient2(
low = "dodgerblue",
mid = "slateblue2",
high = "red",
midpoint = 0
) +
  
# Add labels and a title to the plot
  labs(
    title = "Correlation Between Music Genres and Political Polarization",
    x = "Music Genre / Political Polarization Variable",
    y = "Music Genre / Political Polarization Variable",
    fill = "Correlation Strength"
  ) +
  
# Apply a clean visual theme to the plot
  theme_minimal()

# Convert the ggplot heatmap into an interactive visualization using plotly
# This allows users to hover over tiles to see the exact correlation values
ggplotly(heatmap)
```

This heat map shows the strength and direction of correlations between the number of Billboard Hot 100 number-one songs in five genres (Rock, Pop, Funk, Hip Hop, Electronic) and the U.S. political polarization score. Tiles that are more red indicate a positive correlation, tiles that are more blue indicate a negative correlation, and purple tiles indicate little or no correlation. The intensity of the color reflects how strong the correlation is; darker colors mean stronger relationships. The x-axis and y-axis show the same variables because the heat map displays a correlation matrix, meaning every variable is compared with every other variable in the dataset. Each square represents the relationship between the variable on the x-axis and the variable on the y-axis. Correlation values range from -1 to 1, where values closer to 1 indicate a strong positive relationship, values closer to -1 indicate a strong negative relationship, and values closer to 0 indicate a weak or no relationship. From the heat map, we can see that Rock and Hip Hop have the strongest negative correlations with each other, and both show moderate correlations with political polarization, while Electronic has almost no correlation. This visualization helps quickly identify which genres’ chart success is most associated with changes in political polarization over time.
::::

# Conclusion {scrolling="false"}

::: card
## A conclusion of our major takeaways

Three major takeaways from this project are first that, in contrast with our original hypothesis, Rock music from the Billboard Top 100 Songs was the genre that had the strongest negative correlation and inverse relationship with Political Polarization. Based on the third visualization, the correlation value between Rock music and political polarization is -0.620. The second takeaway is that most genres from the correlation matrix do not have positive correlations with political polarization, except slightly for Hip Hop, which had a correlation value of 0.435. Finally, the diversity of music in the United States is difficult to capture simply and represents many different genres.

Our data exploration is biased because we were unable to study in depth the nuances of music genres and styles across the Billboard Top 100 songs. Since we were limited by our knowledge of R programming, time, and capacity, only the top five prevalent genre counts were represented in the “Discogs Genre” column. The top five genres were Rock, Pop, Hip Hop, Funk, and Electronic, which were selected out of the 80+ distinct labels, including joint genre labels. Therefore, our data exploration is biased towards these top five genres and simplifies the scope of analysis able to be done on the relationship between Political Polarization and Genres.

A future direction our group could take to better understand our big picture question of “How do trends in popular music and music culture relate to changes in political climates in the United States” is to explore another variable other than political polarization such as possibly political party popularity, voter engagement, non-partisan policy achievements, public opinion polls, and a lot more Although Political Polarization was an interesting variable to explore, it can be difficult to communicate to other people, especially non-Americans or people who are not as engaged with American politics. American politics is complex and bureaucratic, meaning that no single variable could fully encompass enough data to answer the question. Additionally, it is worth noting that popular music that performs well on the Billboard Top 100 charts may not necessarily feature themes of political advocacy. Not every popular song represents American protest music, which itself is considered a genre. The different ways that audiences in America are engaged with politics have also evolved, with calls to action being made in film, social media, and journalism.
:::

# Group Info {scrolling="false"}

:::::: columns
::: card
**Bella Vicencio \|** [imov\@uw.edu](mailto:imov@uw.edu) \| Pre-Major (Arts and Sciences)

I am interested in how music trends reflect broader social and political changes in society. I'm especially interested in Informatics because it allows one to use data analysis to explore real-world cultural patterns and connect quantitative research to larger social issues. I am interested in building technical skills in data analysis that can support a future career working with data and research in social or cultural fields.
:::

::: card
**Suler Lu** \| [slu88\@uw.edu](slu88@uw.edu) \| Environmental Studies and Public Health-Global Health

I am interested in how data and data visualization can strengthen public understanding and trust in science and academia. The topic of music and culture as connected to American politics is interesting because it is something I have never had the opportunity to study before!
:::

::: card
**Matthew Reiling \|** [mreil06\@uw.edu](mailto:mreil06@uw.edu) \| Pre-Major (Arts and Sciences)

I have always loved listening to music and is something that I spend many hours every day doing. However, music is often about more than just the listening experience and can tell stories that reflect the times and the environment the author lives or has lived in which I find very intriguing. I am interested in pursuing computer science or informatics so that I can use my passion for technology to discover more about the world we live in and find positive ways to change the way we interact with the world around us.
:::
::::::

::: {.card fill="false"}
## Coding Notes

For this project, we primarily used tools covered in class especially the Lecture and Lab slideshows which included links to "cheat sheets". Other tools used included R, RStudio, and tidyverse packages such as dplyr and ggplot2 for data cleaning, grouping, joining, and visualization. Generative AI was used in a few instances to ask about sample code for solving generic problems such as how to position two scatter plots next to/on top of each other for a stronger visual. No assignment code was shared.
:::
